state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: Project management SaaS with Kanban boards, Gantt charts, time tracking, resource allocation, sprint planning, and Slack/Jira integration.

plan.description: Analyze the project management SaaS market: competitors (Asana, Monday.com, Linear, Jira, ClickUp), pricing models, feature gaps, and target segments (SMB vs enterprise). Identify positioning opportunities.
acceptance_criteria: Competitor matrix with feature comparison across 6+ tools
Pricing benchmark for 3 tiers (free, pro, enterprise)
Identified top 3 positioning differentiators
Target ICP (Ideal Customer Profile) defined

plan.description: Define business requirements and user stories for all core modules: Kanban boards, Gantt charts, time tracking, resource allocation, sprint planning, Slack integration, and Jira integration. Produce prioritized user story map.
acceptance_criteria: User stories written for all 7 modules with acceptance criteria
Process flow diagrams for core workflows (project creation, sprint cycle, time log)
MoSCoW prioritization applied to all stories
ROI analysis document with projected time-savings per user persona
Pricing tiers defined
Churn risk factors identified

plan.description: Produce the Product Requirements Document (PRD), feature roadmap (v1 MVP, v2, v3), success metrics (activation, retention, NPS, MRR), and go/no-go criteria for each milestone.
acceptance_criteria: PRD document with feature specs for all MVP modules
Roadmap with milestones, owners, and dates
Success metrics defined with baseline and target values
Go/no-go criteria documented for each release

plan.description: Build a financial model covering SaaS pricing tiers, COGS (compute, storage, third-party APIs), CAC, LTV, burn rate, and 3-year P&L projection. Include Slack and Jira API cost modeling.
acceptance_criteria: 3-tier pricing model with feature gates defined
COGS breakdown per active user per month
3-year P&L model with sensitivity analysis
Break-even analysis and runway calculation
MRR/ARR projections modeled
CAC and LTV estimated

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, DPA (Data Processing Agreement), and SLA. Review Slack and Jira API terms for redistribution and data residency constraints.
acceptance_criteria: ToS and Privacy Policy drafted and reviewed
DPA template covering GDPR Article 28 obligations
SLA with uptime commitments (99.9%) and credit structure
Legal risk assessment for Slack/Jira API usage documented
Subscription terms cover cancellation and refunds
Data processing agreement drafted

plan.description: Conduct user research interviews with 10 target users. Produce information architecture, wireframes for all major views (board, Gantt, sprint, resource grid, time log, dashboard), and an accessibility audit checklist (WCAG 2.1 AA).
acceptance_criteria: User research synthesis report with 5+ validated pain points
Complete wireframes for all 8 views reviewed by 3 stakeholders
High-fidelity Figma mockups with design tokens (color, spacing, typography)
Accessibility checklist with all critical items passing
Mobile-responsive layouts defined

plan.description: Produce SOC 2 Type II readiness artifacts: control mapping, trust service criteria matrix (Security, Availability, Confidentiality), evidence collection plan, and vendor risk register for Slack and Jira integrations.
acceptance_criteria: SOC 2 control matrix mapped to all trust service criteria
Evidence collection plan with owner and frequency per control
Vendor risk register for all third-party integrations
Gap analysis identifying controls not yet implemented

plan.description: Design the full OpenAPI 3.1 specification for all backend endpoints: projects, tasks, boards, sprints, time entries, resources, users, workspaces, webhooks, and integration connectors (Slack, Jira). Define versioning strategy (v1) and rate limiting contracts.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI 3.1 spec covering all resource endpoints with request/response schemas
Authentication flows documented (OAuth2 + API key)
Webhook event catalog defined (task.created, sprint.started, etc.)
Rate limiting and pagination contracts specified
Integration endpoint contracts for Slack and Jira defined

plan.description: Design the multi-tenant PostgreSQL schema: workspaces, users, projects, boards, columns, tasks, subtasks, sprints, time_entries, resource_allocations, comments, attachments, integrations, audit_log. Include migration scripts and seed data.
acceptance_criteria: ERD diagram with all tables and relationships
Migration scripts for all tables with up/down
Multi-tenant isolation verified (schema-per-tenant pattern)
Seed data scripts for demo workspace
Index strategy documented for high-query paths

plan.description: Set up the monorepo structure (Turborepo), environment configuration, Docker Compose for local development (PostgreSQL, Redis, API, worker, frontend), pre-commit hooks, ESLint/Prettier/Ruff configs, and secret management via dotenv vault.
acceptance_criteria: Monorepo bootstraps with single `pnpm install` command
Docker Compose `up` starts full local stack in under 60 seconds
Pre-commit hooks run lint and type-check on changed files
Environment variable schema validated at startup
README with 5-minute local setup instructions

plan.description: Build the core FastAPI backend: workspace and project CRUD, user auth (JWT + OAuth2), multi-tenant middleware, task management (create/update/delete/reorder), board column management, comments, file attachments (S3), and audit logging.
acceptance_criteria: All CRUD endpoints pass integration tests
JWT auth with refresh token rotation working
RBAC enforced (owner, admin, member, viewer roles)
File upload to S3 with presigned URL pattern
Audit log entries written for all write operations
Multi-tenant isolation verified in all queries

plan.description: Build the Kanban board engine: column ordering, task drag-and-drop position persistence (fractional indexing), WIP limits, swimlanes, board filters, and board templates. Implement real-time updates via WebSocket.
acceptance_criteria: Task reorder persists correct fractional index positions
WIP limit enforced with blocking UI feedback
WebSocket broadcasts task moves to all connected board users in < 100ms
Board filter (by assignee, label, due date) returns correct results
3 built-in templates (Scrum, Kanban, Bug Tracker) available

plan.description: Build the Gantt chart engine: task dependency graph (FS/SS/FF/SF), critical path calculation, date constraint handling, baseline snapshots, and milestone tracking. Expose timeline data endpoint optimized for large projects (1000+ tasks).
acceptance_criteria: Critical path calculated correctly for projects with 100+ tasks
All 4 dependency types enforced with cycle detection
Baseline snapshot creation and diff comparison working
Timeline endpoint returns in < 200ms for 1000 tasks
Milestone markers render with correct date alignment

plan.description: Build the time tracking module: manual time entry, timer start/stop, time log per task/user/project, weekly timesheets, approval workflow, and billable hours flagging. Include reporting aggregations.
acceptance_criteria: Timer start/stop records accurate duration to the second
Manual entry with date/duration/description validation
Timesheet approval workflow with manager notification
Billable hours report exportable as CSV and PDF
Time logged reflected in task progress percentage

plan.description: Build the sprint planning module: sprint creation/archival, backlog management, story point estimation, velocity tracking, sprint capacity calculation, and burndown/burnup chart data endpoints.
acceptance_criteria: Sprint creation with start/end date and capacity in story points
Backlog drag-to-sprint assignment working
Velocity calculated from last 3 completed sprints
Burndown chart data endpoint returns daily remaining points
Sprint archive preserves all historical data

plan.description: Build the resource allocation module: team member availability calendar, capacity heatmap, allocation per project/sprint, over-allocation detection, and workload balancing suggestions.
acceptance_criteria: Availability calendar reflects holidays and time-off correctly
Allocation CRUD stores hours per person per project per date range
Over-allocation alert fires when utilization > 100%
Capacity heatmap endpoint returns per-user utilization by week
Workload summary report lists all over-allocated members

plan.description: Build the Slack integration: OAuth2 app installation, slash commands (/task, /sprint, /time), incoming webhooks for notifications (task assigned, sprint started, deadline approaching), and interactive Block Kit approval for task creation.
acceptance_criteria: Slack OAuth2 install flow completes and stores workspace token
All 4 slash commands return correct responses < 3s
Notification webhooks fire within 5s of triggering event
Block Kit modal creates task and reflects in board immediately
Uninstall clears all stored tokens and stops notifications

plan.description: Build the Jira integration: OAuth2 connection, bi-directional sync (import Jira issues as tasks, push status updates back), field mapping configuration, webhook listener for Jira events, and conflict resolution strategy.
acceptance_criteria: Jira OAuth2 connection established and token refreshed automatically
Import of Jira project creates matching tasks in correct board columns
Status change in app pushes to Jira within 10s
Field mapping UI allows custom mapping configuration
Sync conflicts logged to audit trail with resolution recorded

plan.description: Implement the AI assistant using the coordinator agentic pattern: a central coordinator routes to specialist agents (task decomposer, sprint planner, workload balancer) to provide suggestions. The system surfaces smart recommendations (task prioritization, sprint capacity warnings, bottleneck detection) but requires user approval before applying changes — human_in_the_loop pattern at the action layer.
acceptance_criteria: Coordinator routes sprint planning queries to sprint_capacity_advisor
Over-allocation event triggers workload_balancer suggestion within 5s
All AI-suggested changes presented for user approval before applying
Suggestion acceptance/rejection logged to audit trail
AI suggestion accuracy tracked and reported in analytics

plan.description: Build the React 18 + TypeScript frontend shell: routing (React Router v6), global state (Zustand), API client (TanStack Query), design system (Radix UI + Tailwind), auth flows (login, OAuth, invite), workspace switcher, and responsive layout.
acceptance_criteria: Auth flow (signup → email verify → workspace create) completes end-to-end
OAuth login with Google and GitHub working
Workspace switcher renders all workspaces and switches without full reload
Responsive layout adapts correctly on 320px, 768px, 1280px, 1920px breakpoints
Design system tokens match Figma spec (colors, spacing, typography)

plan.description: Build the Kanban board UI: drag-and-drop columns and cards (dnd-kit), task card component with assignee avatar, labels, due date, story points, WIP limit indicators, inline card editing, board filters sidebar, and real-time update integration via WebSocket.
acceptance_criteria: Card drag between columns updates position optimistically and confirms via WebSocket
Inline card title edit saves on blur without modal
Card detail drawer shows all fields, comments, and time log
WIP limit exceeded shows red column header and blocks drop
Board filter by assignee, label, and due date works in < 50ms

plan.description: Build the Gantt chart UI: interactive timeline (DHTMLX Gantt or custom Canvas renderer), task bars with drag-to-resize and drag-to-move, dependency arrows, critical path highlighting, zoom levels (day/week/month/quarter), and baseline overlay.
acceptance_criteria: Timeline renders 500 tasks at 60fps on mid-range hardware
Drag-to-move updates task dates and cascades dependent tasks
Dependency arrows render between linked tasks at all zoom levels
Critical path highlighted in red
Baseline overlay toggle shows original vs current schedule

plan.description: Build the sprint planning UI: sprint board with backlog panel, story point badges, velocity widget, capacity bar, burndown/burnup charts (Recharts), sprint creation wizard, and retrospective notes editor.
acceptance_criteria: Drag from backlog to sprint updates capacity bar in real time
Burndown chart updates daily with actual vs ideal line
Velocity widget shows last 3 sprint averages
Sprint creation wizard validates dates and capacity before save
Retrospective notes auto-save on blur

plan.description: Build the time tracking UI: floating timer widget (global, persists across navigation), time log list view per task and per user, weekly timesheet grid, approval inbox for managers, and billable hours report with date range picker.
acceptance_criteria: Floating timer persists across route navigation
Timer resumes correctly after page refresh (synced from server)
Weekly timesheet grid allows inline duration editing
Manager approval inbox shows pending entries with one-click approve/reject
CSV export downloads correctly filtered time data

plan.description: Build the resource allocation UI: team capacity heatmap grid (member × week), allocation drag-to-assign, over-allocation highlight (red cells), availability calendar modal, and workload summary dashboard widget.
acceptance_criteria: Heatmap renders 20 team members × 12 weeks grid without performance issues
Over-allocated cells shown in red with tooltip showing breakdown
Drag allocation from project card to team member cell updates correctly

plan.description: Build the integrations settings page: Slack connect flow (OAuth button → success state → channel configuration), Jira connect flow (OAuth → project selector → field mapping UI), integration status badges, and disconnect/re-authorize flows.
acceptance_criteria: Slack OAuth flow completes in app without external redirect issues

Field mapping UI saves custom mappings and persists on reload
Integration status badge shows last sync time and error state

plan.description: Build the analytics dashboard: project health overview, task completion trends (line chart), team velocity over time, time tracking summary, open blockers count, and exportable project status report (PDF).

All charts correctly reflect current database state

Date range filter applies to all dashboard widgets simultaneously

plan.description: Conduct security review: threat model for multi-tenant data isolation, OWASP Top 10 assessment, OAuth token storage and rotation audit, SQL injection / IDOR review, rate limiting verification, and SBOM generation.
acceptance_criteria: Threat model document covering all 4 scope areas
OWASP Top 10 assessment with no Critical findings unmitigated

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions): lint → test → build → docker → deploy. Set up staging and production environments on AWS (ECS Fargate, RDS Aurora PostgreSQL, ElastiCache Redis, S3, CloudFront). Configure monitoring (Datadog), alerting, and SRE runbooks.
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline runs on every PR and blocks merge on failure

acceptance_criteria: Unit test coverage >= 80% for all backend business logic
All API endpoint integration tests pass against real test DB

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system tests: full sprint lifecycle (create → plan → execute → complete), Slack-triggered task creation flowing to board, Jira sync round-trip, time tracking approval workflow, and load test (500 concurrent users, 10k tasks per workspace).
acceptance_criteria: Full sprint lifecycle E2E test passes in < 2 minutes

acceptance_criteria: Evidence folder organized by SOC 2 trust service criteria

All third-party vendor risk assessments completed

acceptance_criteria: API reference auto-generated from OpenAPI spec with example payloads

Jira integration guide walkthrough tested by non-developer
Self-hosting guide: user can deploy from scratch following docs in < 2 hours

plan.description: Produce user onboarding materials: in-app interactive onboarding checklist (7 steps), help center articles for each module, video script outlines for Kanban, Gantt, and sprint planning tutorials, and admin setup guide.
acceptance_criteria: In-app onboarding checklist implemented and dismissable
7 help center articles published and searchable
Video script outlines approved by product manager
Admin setup guide covers workspace creation to first sprint in < 15 minutes
Onboarding completion rate measurable via analytics event tracking

flaws: Schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL hits pg_class and connection bloat at ~500-1000 tenants. For SMB SaaS targeting hundreds of customers, this ceiling will be reached in year 1. Row-level security (RLS) is the correct pattern for this scale, and there is no migration path defined from schema-per-tenant to RLS.

Step 22 defers the Gantt renderer decision to 'DHTMLX or custom Canvas' — this is a 2-4 month wildcard. DHTMLX Gantt commercial license costs $800+/dev and has redistribution restrictions. A custom Canvas Gantt with correct dependency arrow routing, zoom levels, and 60fps for 500 tasks is one of the hardest UI problems in this stack. Treating it as a parenthetical is a critical planning failure.

Fractional indexing (step 12) without a rebalancing strategy will cause floating-point precision exhaustion after thousands of inserts between the same two positions. The acceptance criteria don't require a rebalancing procedure, so this technical debt ships to production.

Step 31 (write tests) depends on steps 12–18 — tests are authored AFTER all backend modules are complete. This is pure waterfall. No TDD, no integration tests written alongside feature code, no early regression signal. By the time tests are written, architectural mistakes in steps 12–18 are fully baked in.

Jira bidirectional sync (step 18) is severely underspecified. Jira Cloud webhooks miss events under load, field mappings between Jira and custom schemas drift silently, and Atlassian's OAuth 2.0 (3LO) token refresh behavior differs by plan. No retry queue, no sync-state reconciliation job, no backfill strategy for missed events. 'Last-write-wins with audit' is not a conflict strategy — it is conflict acknowledgment after the fact.

SOC 2 Type II requires a 6–12 month observation period of implemented controls. Step 34 treats evidence collection as a post-build artifact gathering exercise. Controls must be live and auditable from day one of the observation window. The plan has no start-of-observation trigger, meaning the SOC 2 audit readiness target cannot be met on this linear timeline regardless of how well the other steps execute.

No billing/subscription implementation step exists anywhere in 36 steps. Pricing tiers are defined (steps 2–4), financial models built (step 4), but there is no Stripe integration, no seat counting, no subscription lifecycle management, no dunning logic, and no usage metering. A SaaS product without a billing system cannot collect revenue.

Step 17 specifies Slack notification delivery but does not account for Slack API rate limits: Tier 1 is 1 req/min, Tier 2 is 20 req/min. A workspace with 50 users and active sprint activity will hit these limits immediately. No message queuing, batching, or backpressure strategy is defined.

The WebSocket conflict resolution strategy ('last-write-wins with optimistic locking') is inadequate for collaborative board editing. Two users reordering cards simultaneously will silently discard one user's changes. No CRDT or operational transform approach is considered, and the acceptance criteria don't test concurrent edit scenarios.

Step 19 hardcodes 'claude-sonnet-4-6' as the LLM provider with no fallback, no per-tenant cost controls, no rate limiting on AI inference calls, and no context window management for workspaces with 1000+ tasks. A single large project's task list can exceed the model's context window, causing silent truncation or errors with no graceful degradation.

Timer persistence (step 24) uses 'localStorage + server sync' with no defined conflict resolution. If the server timer and localStorage timer diverge (browser crash, offline usage, multi-tab), the plan provides no tiebreaker. Time tracking errors in a billing-adjacent feature are a support and trust liability.

Steps 1–7 are pure research and documentation before a single line of production code is written. In a market with Asana, Linear, Monday.com, and ClickUp all shipping weekly, this waterfall front-load means 3–4 months of zero validated learning. Critical assumptions about positioning and ICP remain untested until the product ships.
suggestions: Replace schema-per-tenant with row-level security (RLS) on a shared schema. Use a `workspace_id` column on all tables with `CREATE POLICY` statements. This scales to millions of tenants without connection pool exhaustion and is supported natively by Supabase if you want managed RLS.

Make a hard architectural decision on the Gantt renderer in step 3 (PRD), not step 22. Evaluate react-gantt-chart, Bryntum Gantt (commercial, production-grade), or DHTMLX with a legal review of redistribution terms. Spike the renderer before committing to Canvas-from-scratch.

Add a step between 10 and 11 for fractional indexing implementation and rebalancing strategy. Use the Lexorank algorithm (Jira's own approach) or the `fractional-indexing` npm package, and schedule a background rebalancing job that fires when gap density drops below a threshold.

Flip steps 31 and 11–18: write integration test scaffolding in step 10 alongside config setup, and require each backend step (12–18) to ship with its own tests. Gate step 32 on passing tests, not on test authorship.

Add a step 10.5: Billing Integration (Stripe). Implement subscription creation, seat-based metering, webhook handlers for payment failures, and a customer portal link. This is critical path for revenue and should precede any marketing spend.

Add a step 10.6: Transactional Email System. Integrate SES or SendGrid for task assignment notifications, sprint reminders, timesheet approval requests, and password reset. Slack notifications (step 17) are a secondary channel, not a replacement.

Add a GDPR/CCPA implementation step (not just document drafting). Right-to-erasure requires a `DELETE /users/{id}/data` endpoint that cascades deletion across all tenant schemas while preserving anonymized audit log entries. Data portability requires a `GET /export` endpoint. These are legal obligations, not documentation tasks.

For the Slack integration, implement a notification queue (Redis-backed with Celery) that batches and rate-limits outbound messages per workspace, with exponential backoff on 429 responses and a dead-letter queue for undeliverable notifications.

Add Celery Flower or Grafana + Prometheus worker metrics to step 29. Background job failure visibility is not optional when time tracking, Jira sync, and AI suggestions all run async — silent job failures will surface as user complaints, not alerts.

Start the SOC 2 Type II observation period on day one of staging deployment (step 29), not after step 34. Hire a compliance consultant or use Vanta/Drata to instrument controls automatically from the first deployment. Document this explicitly in step 7.

Add a feature flag system (step 10.5 or as part of step 10). LaunchDarkly or a simple database-backed flag table. Step 19 (AI features) and step 18 (Jira sync) are high-risk — they need kill switches without a redeploy.

Step 9 should document a connection pooling strategy: PgBouncer in transaction mode for the API tier, with a separate long-lived connection pool for Celery workers. Without this, schema-per-tenant search_path switching will cause connection exhaustion under load.
missing: Billing and subscription management: Stripe integration, seat counting, upgrade/downgrade flows, dunning, invoice generation. Zero coverage in 36 steps.

Email notification system: no transactional email provider, no email templates, no delivery tracking. Slack is a secondary channel — email is the primary async notification channel for most business users.
GDPR/CCPA implementation (not just documents): right-to-erasure endpoint, data portability export, consent management, data retention policies enforced in code.
Data import/migration tooling: no step for importing from Asana, Jira, Monday.com, or CSV. This is a top-3 adoption blocker for SMB customers switching tools.
Audit log partitioning and archival strategy: the audit_log table will grow at O(write_operations) indefinitely. No time-based partitioning, no archival job, no retention policy.
File upload security controls: no virus scanning (ClamAV or third-party), no MIME type validation beyond extension, no per-workspace storage quota enforcement.
PgBouncer or equivalent connection pooling configuration: missing from both step 9 (schema design) and step 10 (infra config).

Webhook signature verification implementation: Slack signs payloads with HMAC-SHA256, Jira uses a secret token header. Steps 17 and 18 define the webhook receiver but the acceptance criteria don't require signature verification to be tested.
Multi-region or data residency strategy: step 5 flags GDPR data residency as a compliance concern, but no infrastructure step addresses EU vs US data placement for enterprise customers.
Soft-delete and data recovery: no mention of soft-delete (`deleted_at` column) for tasks, projects, or workspaces. Accidental deletion with no recovery path is a support and churn risk.

AI cost guardrails: no per-workspace monthly AI call budget, no token usage tracking, no circuit breaker when LLM provider is down. Step 19 can run unbounded inference costs against a single LLM provider.
security_risks: Schema-per-tenant with dynamic `SET search_path = {tenant_name}` is vulnerable to search_path injection if tenant names are not strictly sanitized (alphanumeric + underscore only). A tenant named `public; DROP SCHEMA public` would be catastrophic. No sanitization requirement is stated in step 9.

Zustand global state store (step 20): if the store is configured with persistence middleware (common pattern), OAuth access tokens may be written to localStorage, directly contradicting the step 28 requirement that tokens never live in localStorage. This is an implementation gap that will likely be introduced by a developer following standard Zustand patterns.

Jira OAuth 2.0 tokens stored in the database need field-level encryption, not just encryption-at-rest on the RDS volume. An application-level SQL injection that dumps the integrations table would expose all Jira tokens in plaintext. No field-level encryption requirement exists in step 9 or 18.

S3 file attachments (step 11): presigned URLs with long expiry times (>1 hour) combined with no file type validation create a stored XSS vector if SVG or HTML files are uploaded and served with permissive Content-Type headers. CloudFront distribution must set `Content-Disposition: attachment` for all non-image types.

AI assistant (step 19) ingests task titles, descriptions, and comments as LLM context. A malicious user can embed prompt injection payloads in task descriptions that alter the AI's suggestions for other users in the same workspace. No input sanitization or prompt injection defense is specified.

Webhook endpoints for Slack and Jira are unauthenticated HTTP receivers. Without HMAC signature verification enforced at the middleware level (not just recommended in docs), these endpoints are open to spoofed event injection — an attacker can fake a Jira issue_deleted event to remove tasks from boards.

Redis pub/sub for WebSocket broadcasts (step 12): if channel naming uses workspace/tenant ID without cryptographic validation, a client that guesses another tenant's channel name could subscribe and receive cross-tenant real-time updates. Channel names must be unguessable (UUID-based), not sequential integers.

Rate limiting is specified at 1000 req/min per tenant (step 8) but no step implements it. Step 28 only verifies it. If the implementation step is missing, the verification step tests a control that was never built.
final_review.summary: This is an ambitious, well-structured plan with thoughtful dependency ordering and appropriate technology choices for most components. However, it has three critical-path failures that would prevent a viable SaaS business from operating: (1) there is no billing implementation — the product cannot charge customers; (2) SOC 2 Type II is fundamentally misunderstood as an artifact collection exercise rather than a 6-12 month control observation period, making the compliance target unachievable on this timeline; and (3) the schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL approach will hit a hard scaling ceiling well within the first year of SMB customer growth. Beyond these blockers, the Gantt renderer ambiguity is a months-long wildcard, the test-after-build waterfall structure bakes in architectural debt, and the absence of email notifications, GDPR implementation, data import tooling, and feature flags represent significant gaps for a production SaaS. The security posture is adequate in intent but has concrete implementation gaps — particularly around prompt injection, search_path sanitization, and token storage — that will be introduced by developers following standard framework patterns. Score of 54 reflects a plan that would produce a functional demo but not a shippable, revenue-generating, compliance-ready SaaS product without substantial rework in the billing, compliance, and data architecture layers.

flaws: Schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL hits pg_class and connection bloat at ~500-1000 tenants. For SMB SaaS targeting hundreds of customers, this ceiling will be reached in year 1. Row-level security (RLS) is the correct pattern for this scale, and there is no migration path defined from schema-per-tenant to RLS.

Step 22 defers the Gantt renderer decision to 'DHTMLX or custom Canvas' — this is a 2-4 month wildcard. DHTMLX Gantt commercial license costs $800+/dev and has redistribution restrictions. A custom Canvas Gantt with correct dependency arrow routing, zoom levels, and 60fps for 500 tasks is one of the hardest UI problems in this stack. Treating it as a parenthetical is a critical planning failure.

Fractional indexing (step 12) without a rebalancing strategy will cause floating-point precision exhaustion after thousands of inserts between the same two positions. The acceptance criteria don't require a rebalancing procedure, so this technical debt ships to production.

Step 31 (write tests) depends on steps 12–18 — tests are authored AFTER all backend modules are complete. This is pure waterfall. No TDD, no integration tests written alongside feature code, no early regression signal. By the time tests are written, architectural mistakes in steps 12–18 are fully baked in.

Jira bidirectional sync (step 18) is severely underspecified. Jira Cloud webhooks miss events under load, field mappings between Jira and custom schemas drift silently, and Atlassian's OAuth 2.0 (3LO) token refresh behavior differs by plan. No retry queue, no sync-state reconciliation job, no backfill strategy for missed events. 'Last-write-wins with audit' is not a conflict strategy — it is conflict acknowledgment after the fact.

SOC 2 Type II requires a 6–12 month observation period of implemented controls. Step 34 treats evidence collection as a post-build artifact gathering exercise. Controls must be live and auditable from day one of the observation window. The plan has no start-of-observation trigger, meaning the SOC 2 audit readiness target cannot be met on this linear timeline regardless of how well the other steps execute.

No billing/subscription implementation step exists anywhere in 36 steps. Pricing tiers are defined (steps 2–4), financial models built (step 4), but there is no Stripe integration, no seat counting, no subscription lifecycle management, no dunning logic, and no usage metering. A SaaS product without a billing system cannot collect revenue.

Step 17 specifies Slack notification delivery but does not account for Slack API rate limits: Tier 1 is 1 req/min, Tier 2 is 20 req/min. A workspace with 50 users and active sprint activity will hit these limits immediately. No message queuing, batching, or backpressure strategy is defined.

The WebSocket conflict resolution strategy ('last-write-wins with optimistic locking') is inadequate for collaborative board editing. Two users reordering cards simultaneously will silently discard one user's changes. No CRDT or operational transform approach is considered, and the acceptance criteria don't test concurrent edit scenarios.

Step 19 hardcodes 'claude-sonnet-4-6' as the LLM provider with no fallback, no per-tenant cost controls, no rate limiting on AI inference calls, and no context window management for workspaces with 1000+ tasks. A single large project's task list can exceed the model's context window, causing silent truncation or errors with no graceful degradation.

Timer persistence (step 24) uses 'localStorage + server sync' with no defined conflict resolution. If the server timer and localStorage timer diverge (browser crash, offline usage, multi-tab), the plan provides no tiebreaker. Time tracking errors in a billing-adjacent feature are a support and trust liability.

Steps 1–7 are pure research and documentation before a single line of production code is written. In a market with Asana, Linear, Monday.com, and ClickUp all shipping weekly, this waterfall front-load means 3–4 months of zero validated learning. Critical assumptions about positioning and ICP remain untested until the product ships.
suggestions: Replace schema-per-tenant with row-level security (RLS) on a shared schema. Use a `workspace_id` column on all tables with `CREATE POLICY` statements. This scales to millions of tenants without connection pool exhaustion and is supported natively by Supabase if you want managed RLS.

Make a hard architectural decision on the Gantt renderer in step 3 (PRD), not step 22. Evaluate react-gantt-chart, Bryntum Gantt (commercial, production-grade), or DHTMLX with a legal review of redistribution terms. Spike the renderer before committing to Canvas-from-scratch.

Add a step between 10 and 11 for fractional indexing implementation and rebalancing strategy. Use the Lexorank algorithm (Jira's own approach) or the `fractional-indexing` npm package, and schedule a background rebalancing job that fires when gap density drops below a threshold.

Flip steps 31 and 11–18: write integration test scaffolding in step 10 alongside config setup, and require each backend step (12–18) to ship with its own tests. Gate step 32 on passing tests, not on test authorship.

Add a step 10.5: Billing Integration (Stripe). Implement subscription creation, seat-based metering, webhook handlers for payment failures, and a customer portal link. This is critical path for revenue and should precede any marketing spend.

Add a step 10.6: Transactional Email System. Integrate SES or SendGrid for task assignment notifications, sprint reminders, timesheet approval requests, and password reset. Slack notifications (step 17) are a secondary channel, not a replacement.

Add a GDPR/CCPA implementation step (not just document drafting). Right-to-erasure requires a `DELETE /users/{id}/data` endpoint that cascades deletion across all tenant schemas while preserving anonymized audit log entries. Data portability requires a `GET /export` endpoint. These are legal obligations, not documentation tasks.

For the Slack integration, implement a notification queue (Redis-backed with Celery) that batches and rate-limits outbound messages per workspace, with exponential backoff on 429 responses and a dead-letter queue for undeliverable notifications.

Add Celery Flower or Grafana + Prometheus worker metrics to step 29. Background job failure visibility is not optional when time tracking, Jira sync, and AI suggestions all run async — silent job failures will surface as user complaints, not alerts.

Start the SOC 2 Type II observation period on day one of staging deployment (step 29), not after step 34. Hire a compliance consultant or use Vanta/Drata to instrument controls automatically from the first deployment. Document this explicitly in step 7.

Add a feature flag system (step 10.5 or as part of step 10). LaunchDarkly or a simple database-backed flag table. Step 19 (AI features) and step 18 (Jira sync) are high-risk — they need kill switches without a redeploy.

Step 9 should document a connection pooling strategy: PgBouncer in transaction mode for the API tier, with a separate long-lived connection pool for Celery workers. Without this, schema-per-tenant search_path switching will cause connection exhaustion under load.
missing: Billing and subscription management: Stripe integration, seat counting, upgrade/downgrade flows, dunning, invoice generation. Zero coverage in 36 steps.

Email notification system: no transactional email provider, no email templates, no delivery tracking. Slack is a secondary channel — email is the primary async notification channel for most business users.
GDPR/CCPA implementation (not just documents): right-to-erasure endpoint, data portability export, consent management, data retention policies enforced in code.
Data import/migration tooling: no step for importing from Asana, Jira, Monday.com, or CSV. This is a top-3 adoption blocker for SMB customers switching tools.
Audit log partitioning and archival strategy: the audit_log table will grow at O(write_operations) indefinitely. No time-based partitioning, no archival job, no retention policy.
File upload security controls: no virus scanning (ClamAV or third-party), no MIME type validation beyond extension, no per-workspace storage quota enforcement.
PgBouncer or equivalent connection pooling configuration: missing from both step 9 (schema design) and step 10 (infra config).

Webhook signature verification implementation: Slack signs payloads with HMAC-SHA256, Jira uses a secret token header. Steps 17 and 18 define the webhook receiver but the acceptance criteria don't require signature verification to be tested.
Multi-region or data residency strategy: step 5 flags GDPR data residency as a compliance concern, but no infrastructure step addresses EU vs US data placement for enterprise customers.
Soft-delete and data recovery: no mention of soft-delete (`deleted_at` column) for tasks, projects, or workspaces. Accidental deletion with no recovery path is a support and churn risk.

AI cost guardrails: no per-workspace monthly AI call budget, no token usage tracking, no circuit breaker when LLM provider is down. Step 19 can run unbounded inference costs against a single LLM provider.
security_risks: Schema-per-tenant with dynamic `SET search_path = {tenant_name}` is vulnerable to search_path injection if tenant names are not strictly sanitized (alphanumeric + underscore only). A tenant named `public; DROP SCHEMA public` would be catastrophic. No sanitization requirement is stated in step 9.

Zustand global state store (step 20): if the store is configured with persistence middleware (common pattern), OAuth access tokens may be written to localStorage, directly contradicting the step 28 requirement that tokens never live in localStorage. This is an implementation gap that will likely be introduced by a developer following standard Zustand patterns.

Jira OAuth 2.0 tokens stored in the database need field-level encryption, not just encryption-at-rest on the RDS volume. An application-level SQL injection that dumps the integrations table would expose all Jira tokens in plaintext. No field-level encryption requirement exists in step 9 or 18.

S3 file attachments (step 11): presigned URLs with long expiry times (>1 hour) combined with no file type validation create a stored XSS vector if SVG or HTML files are uploaded and served with permissive Content-Type headers. CloudFront distribution must set `Content-Disposition: attachment` for all non-image types.

AI assistant (step 19) ingests task titles, descriptions, and comments as LLM context. A malicious user can embed prompt injection payloads in task descriptions that alter the AI's suggestions for other users in the same workspace. No input sanitization or prompt injection defense is specified.

Webhook endpoints for Slack and Jira are unauthenticated HTTP receivers. Without HMAC signature verification enforced at the middleware level (not just recommended in docs), these endpoints are open to spoofed event injection — an attacker can fake a Jira issue_deleted event to remove tasks from boards.

Redis pub/sub for WebSocket broadcasts (step 12): if channel naming uses workspace/tenant ID without cryptographic validation, a client that guesses another tenant's channel name could subscribe and receive cross-tenant real-time updates. Channel names must be unguessable (UUID-based), not sequential integers.

Rate limiting is specified at 1000 req/min per tenant (step 8) but no step implements it. Step 28 only verifies it. If the implementation step is missing, the verification step tests a control that was never built.
history.summary: This is an ambitious, well-structured plan with thoughtful dependency ordering and appropriate technology choices for most components. However, it has three critical-path failures that would prevent a viable SaaS business from operating: (1) there is no billing implementation — the product cannot charge customers; (2) SOC 2 Type II is fundamentally misunderstood as an artifact collection exercise rather than a 6-12 month control observation period, making the compliance target unachievable on this timeline; and (3) the schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL approach will hit a hard scaling ceiling well within the first year of SMB customer growth. Beyond these blockers, the Gantt renderer ambiguity is a months-long wildcard, the test-after-build waterfall structure bakes in architectural debt, and the absence of email notifications, GDPR implementation, data import tooling, and feature flags represent significant gaps for a production SaaS. The security posture is adequate in intent but has concrete implementation gaps — particularly around prompt injection, search_path sanitization, and token storage — that will be introduced by developers following standard framework patterns. Score of 54 reflects a plan that would produce a functional demo but not a shippable, revenue-generating, compliance-ready SaaS product without substantial rework in the billing, compliance, and data architecture layers.
